head_coach.name: Dan Campbell
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Physical, aggressive, run-first identity. Former TE who played 11 years and coached TEs in Miami and New Orleans under Sean Payton. Values grit, toughness, 4th-down aggressiveness, and establishing the run. Took over offensive playcalling midseason in 2025 after firing OC John Morton from duties.

offensive_coordinator.name: Drew Petzing
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Kevin Stefanski / Minnesota Vikings tree; Browns (Stefanski) tight ends coach; Arizona Cardinals OC 2023-2025. Run-heavy, play-action oriented, TE-centric passing game.

defensive_coordinator.name: Kelvin Sheppard
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Aaron Glenn disciple / Lions internal promotion. Former LB (LSU/NFL). Coached under Glenn in Detroit's 4-3 aggressive man-heavy system since 2021. First-year DC in 2025, entering second year in 2026.

offense.scheme_family: Run-heavy play-action, Coryell-influenced vertical passing with Stefanski/Browns TE-centric concepts
offense.base_personnel: 11 personnel primary, with significant 12 and 13 personnel usage expected under Petzing. In Arizona, Petzing ran 8th-most 12 personnel and 3rd-most 13 personnel in 2025. Campbell has stated Detroit will not become a heavy-set team like Arizona but will adapt to roster. Expect a blend of 11 and 12 personnel.
offense.run_scheme: Outside zone / wide zone primary, gap and power concepts mixed in. Petzing's Cardinals ranked 2nd in NFL in rushing average (4.92 YPC) across his three seasons. Hank Fraley continues as run game coordinator / OL coach, providing continuity from the dominant 2022-2024 run game.
offense.pass_concepts: Play-action heavy with under-center looks, vertical shots off play-action, high-accuracy short/intermediate routes to complement Jared Goff's strengths. Petzing emphasized TE in the passing game in Arizona (Trey McBride: 126 receptions, 1,239 yards in 2025). Mike Kafka joins as passing game coordinator, adding Erhardt-Perkins / Chiefs-tree concepts.
offense.te_usage: High-priority receiving weapon. Sam LaPorta is a featured pass catcher (was on pace for 76 rec / 924 yards before injury in 2025). Under Petzing in Arizona, Trey McBride averaged 106 receptions per year over three seasons. LaPorta expected to see even bigger role in 2026 under this TE-friendly system. 12 and 13 personnel sets will be utilized.
offense.rb_usage: Featured bell cow in Jahmyr Gibbs. In 2025, Gibbs posted 1,223 rushing yards + 77 receptions for 616 receiving yards and 18 total TDs. David Montgomery traded to Houston. Gibbs is now the clear RB1 with elite dual-threat usage. Petzing values strong RB receiving role in play-action offense.

defense.alignment: Even front, 4-down linemen (two DEs with hands in dirt, including Aidan Hutchinson as primary edge rusher, two DTs). Three off-ball LBs in base. Detroit played the most base 4-3 defense in the NFL in 2025 (~60% of snaps), far above the league average of ~20%.
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-3 DE hands-in-dirt. Aidan Hutchinson is the premier edge rusher as a true defensive end. Marcus Davenport, Al-Quadin Muhammad as complementary DEs. Kacy Rodgers (former Todd Bowles DL coach from Tampa) coaches the DL. Edge rushers are traditional 4-3 DEs, not stand-up OLBs.
defense.coverage_base: Man-heavy with Cover 1 / single-high tendencies, zone mixed in. Aaron Glenn's aggressive man-coverage system was largely maintained by Sheppard. Heavy reliance on man coverage with press concepts. Sheppard intends to continue this identity in Year 2.
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate to heavy (aggressive pressure packages inherited from Glenn system)
defense.lb_role: Traditional run-stopping LBs who must also cover. MIKE (Jack Campbell) is the defensive quarterback — sideline-to-sideline tackler who also added 3 sacks in 2025. WILL (Alex Anzalone, pending free agency) is the backside pursuit LB. SAM (Derrick Barnes) is a hybrid edge-setting/pass rush LB deployed outside the tackle box on the LOS, with responsibilities including rushing the passer, overloading the box, and dropping into coverage. SAM role creates some LB→pass rush value.
defense.safety_usage: Dual-safety system with Brian Branch and Kerby Joseph as elite starters. Branch is a true hybrid — played nickel as a rookie, moved to safety, logged 2.5 sacks and showed versatile blitz/coverage/box skills in 2025 before tearing his Achilles. Joseph is a deep ball-hawk safety. Both suffered significant injuries in 2025; safety is a major draft need for 2026. Sheppard values the ability to dictate to offenses by staying in base and maintaining physicality at the LB/safety level.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: This is one of the highest TE-value offenses in the NFL for 2026. New OC Drew Petzing's track record is definitive: in Arizona, Trey McBride averaged 106 receptions per year over three seasons and earned back-to-back Pro Bowls, including a First-Team All-Pro with 126 catches and 1,239 yards in 2025. Petzing's offense features TEs as primary receiving weapons in 12/13 personnel with play-action, seam routes, and flexed alignments. Sam LaPorta already profiles as an elite receiving TE (86 rec, 10 TDs as a rookie). Detroit's offense will treat a receiving TE like a WR1B-level target. A TE prospect with elite route-running, hands, and separation gets maximum value here.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Detroit runs a true 4-3 with hands-in-dirt DEs as the primary edge rushers — Aidan Hutchinson is the archetype. LBs are NOT the primary pass rushers as they would be in a 3-4. HOWEVER, the SAM linebacker role (Derrick Barnes) does function as a hybrid edge-setter/rusher deployed on the LOS outside the tackle box with pass-rush responsibilities. This creates a narrow pathway for an LB with pass-rush traits to contribute as a SAM, but the primary edge rush still comes from the DE position. Traditional ILBs (MIKE/WILL) rarely rush the passer. A pass-rushing LB prospect would have limited but real value in the SAM role specifically.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Jahmyr Gibbs is the prototype: 1,223 rushing yards PLUS 77 receptions for 616 receiving yards in 2025 — career-highs in targets (94), catches, and receiving yards. Detroit's offense under Campbell has always valued backs who catch (Gibbs + Montgomery were both used in the passing game). Petzing's play-action system further elevates RBs who can run routes out of the backfield. With Montgomery traded, Detroit may seek a complementary back who can also catch. Any RB prospect with elite hands, route-running, and pass-protection skills gets a significant boost here.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Detroit's defensive identity is built around versatile safeties. Brian Branch is the gold standard: he was drafted as a nickel CB, moved to safety, and logged 2.5 sacks, 75 tackles, and 9 passes defended while playing box, slot, and deep roles in 2025 before a torn Achilles. Kerby Joseph is also injured (knee). Both are facing uncertain 2026 timelines. Safety is the Lions' most critical draft need. Sheppard's heavy-base defense (60% 4-3 base, lowest nickel rate in NFL) demands safeties who can function like LBs in the box while also covering. A safety prospect with tackling, blitz ability, man-coverage skills, and positional versatility (box/slot/deep) gets maximum value — Detroit is literally looking for another Brian Branch-type player. Sheppard even referenced wanting a player like Seattle's Nick Emmanwori (6'3, 225-lb hybrid nickel/safety).

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Detroit's 2026 draft profile centers on three premium needs shaped by scheme and injury. (1) SAFETY is the top need: Both Brian Branch (torn Achilles, may miss early 2026) and Kerby Joseph (chronic knee, uncertain future) are injured. Sheppard's defense demands hybrid safeties who can play in the box, cover man-to-man in the slot, and blitz — the team played 60% base 4-3, the most in the NFL. Any safety with versatility (nickel/box/deep) and physicality should be valued significantly higher for DET. Think Emmanwori/Derwin James/Kyle Hamilton archetypes. (2) TIGHT END receiving value is elite here: New OC Drew Petzing's system made Trey McBride a First-Team All-Pro. Sam LaPorta is coming off back surgery, and Detroit could add a versatile TE via the draft. Any TE with receiving upside (route-running, hands, YAC) gets a major boost. (3) RB dual-threat value remains high: Gibbs is the bellcow, but with Montgomery traded, a pass-catching complement is needed. (4) EDGE remains important but comes from the DE position, not stand-up LBs — draft EDGE prospects as traditional DEs. (5) The SAM LB role provides a narrow but real pathway for athletic LBs with rush/coverage hybrid traits. (6) Offensive line is a major need (interior especially) but is position-label neutral. Detroit picks 17th overall in the 2026 draft.
